symptoms.symptom_name: Bright red rectal bleeding confined to the toilet paper after defecation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Bright red blood on wiping, not mixed with stool, is the classic pattern of a distal anorectal (haemorrhoidal) bleed.
Any new rectal bleeding mandates active exclusion of malignancy via examination and screening, even when a benign local cause is likely.
Bright red blood on the paper is not the melaena expected from a significant upper-GI source.

symptoms.symptom_name: New soft perianal nodule at the anal verge

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A palpable/visible lump at the anal verge is the defining feature of an external haemorrhoid.
A fissure is a mucosal tear and does not usually produce an external lump.

symptoms.symptom_name: Onset after straining with a hard stool on a low-fibre diet

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Straining and constipation are the principal mechanical drivers of haemorrhoidal engorgement.
Hard stool and straining can also precipitate an anal fissure.

symptoms.symptom_name: Perianal pruritus and sensation of incomplete evacuation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pruritus ani and a sense of incomplete evacuation are common accompaniments of external haemorrhoids.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of severe tearing pain; lump soft and only mildly tender

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Acute thrombosis produces a tense, hard, dark, exquisitely painful lump — absence of these features argues strongly against it.
A fissure typically causes severe tearing pain on defecation, which is absent here.
Absence of melaena helps exclude a significant upper-GI bleeding source.

symptoms.symptom_name: No unintentional weight loss or change in bowel habit

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of alarm features lowers pre-test probability of malignancy, though it does not remove the indication for examination and screening in new rectal bleeding.

symptoms.symptom_name: Perianal inspection and digital rectal examination findings (soft bluish nodule, normal sphincter tone, empty vault, no internal mass, no blood on glove, no mucosal prolapse on valsalva)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: In-person examination confirms a benign external nodule with no internal mass or active bleeding — sealed clinician finding not obtainable via telehealth; the AI Doctor must arrange in-person review rather than infer it.
Normal DRE with empty vault and no palpable mass is reassuring, but does not assess proximal bowel — screening still required.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal vital signs — afebrile, haemodynamically stable

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal, reassuring observations consistent with a minor bleed and no systemic upset; clinician-measured in the source visit and not obtainable via chat without a home device.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Bright-red bleeding on the paper + a new soft anal-verge lump + onset after straining + absence of severe pain together form a coherent benign uncomplicated external-haemorrhoid picture — the combination is what supports conservative management over escalation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person perianal examination and digital rectal examination by a GP
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms the benign local source and formally assesses for an internal or rectal cause of bleeding — the examination telehealth cannot provide. Routine (within ~2 weeks), not emergency, given the reassuring history.
Assists confirmation by inspecting the internal anal canal; appropriate in the outpatient setting, not acutely indicated over external inspection.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Age-appropriate bowel-cancer screening / colonoscopy referral as indicated
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: New rectal bleeding is an indication to review bowel-cancer risk and arrange screening or colonoscopy per age and risk — the safeguard against missed colorectal malignancy.
Not indicated for an uncomplicated benign anorectal presentation with no alarm features — over-investigation.
